content: In the doc comment of https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20220117053349.6804-6-ojeda@kernel.org/ floating-point functionality is mentioned as an example of unused functionality that needs compiler-builtins intrinsics. This is no longer the case since https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/86048 and those intrinsics are thus not defined anymore. Only 128bit ints and 64bit multiplication/division intrinsics are still defined. The doc comment should probably be updated.
Gary is a rust-for-linux contributor :-) I think the point is that that these cfgs aren't officially supported
I know. It is just that the doc comment doesn't entirely match what the source does anymore.

In https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20220117053349.6804-9-ojeda@kernel.org/ extern "C" fn init_module() { ... } and the other extern "C" functions should probably be marked as unsafe. Rustc doesn't automatically mark them as unsafe. That only happens for extern "C" { ... }, declarations, not for definitions. In addition fn __init() { ... } and fn __exit() { ... } should probably be unsafe. They contain unsynchronized accesses to static mut __MOD and would probably invalidate safety assumptions made by functions using __MOD.
While it isn't very likely someone will accidentally misuse them I think, it is still technically unsound.
Indeed! Would you be interested in sending a PR https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/macros/module.rs
I haven't made my last name public right now. I might in the future, but for now I prefer not to, so I can't sign off on the change.
Ah, interesting. Can you file a bug so we don't forget, and I'll try to get to this later today. For now it's lunch time!

Is it common within the linux kernel community to post big patches like "rust: add kernel crate" (https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20220117053349.6804-10-ojeda@kernel.org/) as a single unit rather than splitting it into multiple independent parts?

In lib/Kconfig.debug of https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20220117053349.6804-16-ojeda@kernel.org/ RUST_OPT_LEVEL_SIMILAR_AS_CHOSEN_FOR_C is mentioned as mapping -Os to -Copt-level=z. This has been changed to -Copt-level=s I believe.

https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/56935 is closed. It is referenced at

+# ICE on `--extern macros`: https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/56935
+$(objtree)/rust/kernel.o: private rustc_target_flags = --extern alloc \
+    --extern build_error \
+    --extern macros=$(objtree)/rust/libmacros.so
I skipped most of "rust: add kernel crate", but otherwise read the entire patch set now.

bjorn3 said:

Is it common within the linux kernel community to post big patches like "rust: add kernel crate" (https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20220117053349.6804-10-ojeda@kernel.org/) as a single unit rather than splitting it into multiple independent parts?

So far, I kept sending it the same way since nobody requested the split, but it is indeed becoming a huge patch, so I may end up splitting it.
Thanks a lot for the review @bjorn3! As usual, it is invaluable -- I will send a PR.
Can I ask a newbie question? I don't see a lot of feedback to patch v3 on lore.kernel.org. Is that a good or bad thing?

It is OK -- different maintainers take a look as they can spare time, i.e. not everyone can spend a lot of time reviewing everything every time (specially as we are ramping up the frequency of submissions).
